preFix.title: Re-webbing a sagging sofa base
preFix.subtitle: Replace broken jute webbing to restore the seat support on a sprung sofa
preFix.excerpt: How to strip and replace the jute webbing on a sagging sofa seat base: removing the old webbing and dust cover, re-lacing the new webbing in an interlaced pattern with a webbing stretcher, and re-stapling the dust cover.

content.text: A sofa that sags in the seat or springs have worked loose is often a webbing failure rather than a spring or foam problem. Replacing the
on the underside of the seat frame is a half-day job and restores the original support without re-upholstering the seat.

Turn the sofa upside down or tilt it forward to access the underside of the seat frame. Remove the dust cover (black fabric on the base) by pulling out all the staples or tacks around its perimeter with the staple remover. Remove all the old webbing runs by pulling the staples along each strap. Count the number of front-to-back runs and side-to-side runs before discarding the old webbing.
Step 2, plan the new webbing layout

Space the webbing runs evenly across the frame, leaving a gap of approximately one webbing width between each run. The correct spacing is narrower for heavier sofas; copy the original spacing if the old webbing is intact enough to count. Cut each webbing length to the frame width plus 75 mm for the folded-end fixings.
Step 3, fix and stretch the webbing

Fold 25 mm of the webbing end over itself and staple through the double layer onto the frame rail with three 10 mm staples in a triangle. Hook the webbing stretcher peg through the other end of the strap, brace the stretcher against the opposite rail, and lever down to tension the webbing. The webbing should feel drum-taut: it should not deflect more than 15 mm under moderate hand pressure. Staple the stretched end through a fold, then release the stretcher and cut. Fit all front-to-back runs first, then weave the side-to-side runs over and under in alternating passes.

Cut new dust cover fabric (a piece of black woven cotton or the original if undamaged) to the underside frame dimensions plus 25 mm on all sides for the turnover. Fold the edges under and staple around the perimeter, pulling the fabric taut. The dust cover is cosmetic; it protects the webbing from below but does not affect seat support.

attrs.fix: Use traditional black-and-white jute webbing rather than synthetic alternatives; re-strip and re-fit with the webbing stretcher to a taut tension.
attrs.cause: Webbing was not tensioned tightly enough during fitting, or cheaper non-jute webbing was used.
attrs.symptom: Seat sags again within a few months of re-webbing

attrs.fix: Use 10 mm staples through a double-thickness fold at each end. Three staples in a triangle provide better resistance than a row of three in line.
attrs.cause: Staples were too short or the fold was too small to give the staples sufficient grip.
attrs.symptom: Webbing pulls away from the frame rail
